coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dot/gold nanoparticles and thiol-containing organic compound for measuring methotrexate anti-cancer drug
coredata.dc:creator: Zamani, Maryam
Tavakkoli, Nahid
Soltani, Nasrin
coredata.dc:description: Methotrexate (MTX) anti-Cancer drug is one of the most important drugs widely used in medicine today; however, overuse of this drug causes many side effects, so it is vital to measure it. Therefore, in this work, a straightforward and inexpensive method was designed to produce a sensitive electrochemical sensor based a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for this drug measurement. The sensor was prepared using graphene quantum dot (GQD), which was synthesized by a green method without using toxic and dangerous substances. Analysis and characterization of the synthesized GQD were performed using different analyzes, and then the modification of the GCE surface was achieved by this GQD. In the second step, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and thiol organic compounds were also placed on GQD by electrochemical method. The prepared sensor showed a good application in MTX measurement comparable to and better than previous works. Significant increase in current at the surface of the modified electrode is due to the high surface area, good conductivity, and electrocatalytic activity of these compounds. These improvements were from the synergistic effect of GQD/AuNPs nanocomposite and the catalytic capability of poly thiol organic compound. The detection limit for MTX was in the linear range of 0.1–100 μM equal to 0.03 μM. The capability of the prepared sensor was investigated in a real sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methotrexate
Graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Organic thiol compound

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM image of GCE, GCE/GQD, GCE/GQD/AuNPs and GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA surface.
TEM image of graphene quantum dot (GQD).
(a). Cyclic voltammograms of different electrodes in the absence (a) and presence (b) of 50 μM MTX in 0.04 M BR solution with pH = 6 and scan rate = 50 mV·s.
Nyquist plots different electrodes in 1 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4- solution.
(a) Cyclic voltammograms of 50 μM MTX at different pH, at GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA surface, (b) current diagram versus pH, (c) potential diagram versus pH.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of MTX at different scan rates at the GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA surface and pH = 6 (b), the current diagram versus the square of scan rate, (c) the potential diagram versus the logarithm of current at scan rate equal to 10 mV·s.
(a) DPVs different concentrations of MTX and (b) calibration curve.
Comparison of the analytical parameters of diverse sensors in MTX determination.
Investigating the effect of disturbance in MTX measurement.

row.entry: sodium chloride, iron nitrate, magnesium sulfate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: MTX determination in plasma sample at GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dot/gold nanoparticles and thiol-containing organic compound for measuring methotrexate anti-cancer drug

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University (PNU), P.O. Box 19395-4697, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University, 19395-4697, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Methotrexate (MTX) anti-Cancer drug is one of the most important drugs widely used in medicine today; however, overuse of this drug causes many side effects, so it is vital to measure it. Therefore, in this work, a straightforward and inexpensive method was designed to produce a sensitive electrochemical sensor based a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for this drug measurement. The sensor was prepared using graphene quantum dot (GQD), which was synthesized by a green method without using toxic and dangerous substances. Analysis and characterization of the synthesized GQD were performed using different analyzes, and then the modification of the GCE surface was achieved by this GQD. In the second step, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and thiol organic compounds were also placed on GQD by electrochemical method. The prepared sensor showed a good application in MTX measurement comparable to and better than previous works. Significant increase in current at the surface of the modified electrode is due to the high surface area, good conductivity, and electrocatalytic activity of these compounds. These improvements were from the synergistic effect of GQD/AuNPs nanocomposite and the catalytic capability of poly thiol organic compound. The detection limit for MTX was in the linear range of 0.1–100 μM equal to 0.03 μM. The capability of the prepared sensor was investigated in a real sample.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Methotrexate (MTX), with a chemical name of 2,4-diamino-N10-methyl pteroylglutamic acid, has long been prescribed by doctors as an anti-cancer drug in the treatment of cancers such as head, and neck cancer, breast cancer and stomach cancer, as well as in the treatment of autoimmune diseases [1–3]. The function of this drug in the body is such that by inhibiting dihydrofolate reductase, it affects the production of tetrahydrofolate and thus inhibits the biochemical synthesis of DNA [4–9]. This drug is highly toxic, and the use of high doses may have adverse effects on the growth of healthy cells. Therefore, research has shown that the clinical use of this drug is limited due to its severe aspect [10]. Therefore, control of MTX concentration in the blood is essential to obtain the appropriate drug dose with the highest therapeutic effect and the lowest toxicity. Due to the high dose levels of this drug and the long duration of treatment, the development of a simple, rapid and cost-effective method for quantitative analysis of active MTX concentrations is crucial. Although various sensitive techniques such as spectrophotometry, electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometry, high-performance liquid chromatography, fluorimetry capillary, and electrophoresis have been used to measure MTX drug, these methods are encountered with limitations such as the heightened analysis time, expensive and complex instrumentation, the operation of the device by capable experts, high organic solvents consumption and complex sample preparation steps [10–14]. To overcome these limitations, electrochemical methods have received high attention due to their advantages, such as Low-cost equipment, simplicity, short analysis times, easy handing, and on-site measurements. In electrochemical methods, by selecting suitable electrocatalytic materials as electrode modifiers, the amount of MTX in biological samples can be measured with high selectivity and sensitivity [15]. Up to date, nanostructures such as carbon nanomaterials (graphene, graphene quantum dots, carbon nanotubes, etc.), metal nanoparticles, conductive polymers and etc., have been used to different uses [16–21] like photocatalytic [22–27] and construct stable electrochemical sensors with high surface area and fast electron transfer (high sensitivity and selectivity) for MTX and other drug detection [3,28–31].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from the graphene family with few-layered graphene sheets and zero-dimensional structure have received great attention in the optical and electrical fields because of possessing excellent properties such as high surface area, π conjugated network for better surface bonds and the high length-to-thickness ratio [32–35]. One of the exciting features of graphene quantum dots is their coupling and proper immobilization with various organic and inorganic species, which is due to the carboxylic functional groups located at the edge (edge effects) [36–38]. Despite its exceptional features, the use of GQDs as electrochemical sensors is still in its infancy and requires a further investigation. Therefore, to increase the performance of electrochemical sensors, their composites can be designed and used, combining GQDs nanoparticles with other nanomaterials [39]. Due to the hydrophilic nature of the surface, which results from the presence of carboxylic and hydroxyl functional groups of GQDs, these GQDs thin layer is better combined with mineral nanoparticles such as metal nanoparticles [40,41]. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are a group of metal nanoparticles that can be quickly synthesized in different sizes and morphologies. After deposition on the electrode surface, these nanoparticles can be caused to increase the effective surface of the electrode, effective mass transfer, and electron transfer due to their spherical morphology, high surface-to-volume ratio as well as metal properties [42–44]. AuNPs have been used in the manufacture of electrochemical sensors due to excellent electrocatalytic properties, high electrical conductivity, high surface area, and biocompatibility. Due to the synergistic effects between nanoparticles and other nanomaterials, by forming nanocomposites of AuNPs nanoparticles, a sensitive substrate can be provided for electrochemical analysis of different analytes in different environmental and biological environments [45–49]. Yang et al. designed the construction of a sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor based on GQD/gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) modified glassy carbon electrode for the determination of quercetin in the biological samples [34]. Based on the electrocatalytic activity, Ounnunkad et al. developed a novel electrochemical biosensor based on a AuNPs/GQDs/graphene oxide film for simultaneous detection breast cancer clinically related microRNAs [39]. Mansuriya and Altintas reported designing a nano-immunosensor based on GQDs/AuNPs for Cardiac Biomarker Determination [50]. The mentioned examples verified the exceptional properties of GQDs and AuNPs for the electrochemical sensors construction. Recently, organic ligands with active functional groups containing of SH, NH and OH have been employed to increase the effective binding sites and deliver direct multivalent interactions with biological molecules. This binding allows Au nanoparticles to be exploited as self-therapeutic agents against bacteria and can alter the surface hydrophobicity of nanoparticles against organic molecules [51–53]. In this work, extensive attention has been focused on the modification of the electrode, with nanoscale materials due to unique features such as great effective surface area, small size efficacy with colossal loading capacity. The sensor proposed in this work has not been reported so far. Modifying the GCE with GQDS/AuNPs/Thiol ligand nanocomposite is beneficial to achieve an expedited detection with enhanced sensitivity and selectivity. So, an electrochemical sensor was made based on modifying a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with GQDS/AuNPs/Thiol ligand nanocomposite. First, GQDs nanosheets were deposited on the electrode surface. Then AuNPs and thiol ligand of 1-Amino- 2-naphthol- 4-sulfonic acid (ANSA) were electrochemically deposited on GQDs, respectively. Thiol was linked to the surface via AuS bonds to affectedly increase the electrochemical response. Thiol ligand, as a functional monomer with hydroxyl and amino groups, has excellent electrochemical stability with good electron transfer capability. Excellent electrocatalytic properties, sensitivity, and selectivity of the newly introduced sensor were demonstrated by the application of this electrode for the measurement of MTX drug in biological solutions.

MTX (99.9 %) was purchased from Mehrdarou Company (Tehran, Iran), Citric acid monohydrate (≥99.9 %), phosphoric acid (85.0 %), acetic acid (100.0 %), ethanol absolute (100 %), boric acid (99.9 %), dimethylformamide (≥99 %), chloroauric acid (37 %), potassium chloride (99.9 %), 1-Amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid (ANSA, 98 %), hexacyanoferrate (99.5 %), and hexacyano ferrite (99.5 %) were purchased from Merck (Germany). Briton-Rubinson (BR) solution was used not only as a supporting electrolyte, but was used for adjustment of pH in the range of 3 to 9. Other reagents purchased from Sigma Company or Merck were in the analytical grade. Distilled deionized water was used for the preparation of aqueous solutions. Autolab PGSTAT-302 (Eco Chemie B. V.) potentiostat/galvanostat as the experimental apparatus for voltammetry. A conventional three-electrode system was used consisting of the bare or modified graphite, an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl), a carbon wire as the working, reference and counter electrodes, respectively. Also, the pH meter of metrohm Switzerland model was used.

GQD was synthesized by hydrothermal method using citric acid without any hazardous solvents. For this purpose, 3.0 g of citric acid was dissolved in water (35 mL), it was stirred for 10 min. The prepared solution was transferred to an autoclave and placed in an oven at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling the autoclave, the obtained product was centrifuged and dried after separation at 50 °C. The resulting product is GQD.

Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode and platinum wire electrode as the opposite electrode was used in electrochemical tests. First, the GCE was washed with water, then polished with alumina powder and then cleaned with water and ethanol. To prepare, the electrode was first scanned in 0.1 M sulfuric acid by cyclic voltammetry (10 scans, 100 mV·s). Then disperse 1 mg of GQD in 1 mL of DMF and place 2 μl of it on the electrode surface and dry at 45 °C. The prepared electrode was called GCE/GQD. This GCE/GQD was placed in a gold solution containing potassium chloride and started cyclic voltammetry in the potential range of 0.2 to −1 V with a scanning rate of 10 mV·s and 10 scans to form gold nanoparticles on the surface. The electrode is removed from the gold solution, washed with water and dried at room temperature. The prepared electrode was called GCE/GQD/AuNPs. The thiol composition was placed on GCE/GQD/AuNPs by cyclic voltammetry method in the potential range of −0.5 to 2 V with a scanning rate of 100 mV·s and 5 scans. Finally the prepared electrode was washed with water and the electrode was called GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA.

Human plasma samples were obtained from a hospital affiliated with Isfahan University of Medical Sciences. To remove plasma proteins, 1 mL of acetonitrile was added to 1 mL of fresh plasma and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to separate protein-free plasma. Then, the MTX measurement was performed on the real sample using the standard addition method.

Electrochemical measurement of MTX was performed on the surface of the prepared sensors by cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry with a micro-model Autolab device and Nova software. A specific concentration of MTX was added to three electrodes cells containing phosphate buffer and its electrochemical behavior was investigated. Differential pulse voltammetry tests were performed with potential amplitude, scan rate and potential steps 1, 3 and 5. The buffer solution was prepared from a mixture of 0.04 M boric acid, acetic acid and phosphoric acid adjusted with 0.2 M sodium hydroxide and used as an electrolyte. MTX solution with a concentration of 1 mM was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of this drug in methanol and diluted with water. All the materials in this work have analytical purity and 1, 2 and 3 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images of the modified electrodes surface are given in Fig. 3 . On the surface of the unmodified GCE there is no compound or substance and an immaculate surface without any contamination is observed that can be modified (Fig. 3a). When the GCE is modified with GQD, graphene quantum dot nanoparticles are visible on the surface (Fig. 3b). When AuNPs are electrochemically deposited on the GCE/GQD surface, the gold nanoparticles are added on the surface in a specific manner, which can be seen in the FE-SEM images (Fig. 3c). Also, the electropolymerization of the thiol group places a thin layer on the electrode, which can be seen in the FE-SEM image of Fig. 3d.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of GQD is given in Fig. 4 . This image shows that the particle size of graphene quantum dot is uniform and is around 20–30 nm. Particles have been demonstrated by gathering GQDs on each other. According to the report of Lee et al., when GQD is formed at high temperatures, the particles are placed on top of each other and the size of the particles increases. Therefore as the temperature rises, the particles become more extensive and are visible in a heterogeneous shape.

Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) without the presence of drug in 0.04 M BR solution are shown on the surface of different GCE, GCE/GQD, GCE/GQD/AuNPs and GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA in Fig. 5a. According to the Fig. 5a no oxidation or reduction peaks are observed on the surface of the electrodes. The observed peak in the presence of MTX can be attributed to the oxidation of the drug. The electrochemical behavior of 50 μM MTX in 0.04 M BR solution on the GCE, GCE/GQD, GCE/GQD/AuNPs and GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA surface was investigated by cyclic voltammetry. The MTX cyclic voltammogram on the surface of the unmodified electrode shows an oxidation peak at a potential of 0.76 V with an oxidation current of 5 μA (Fig. 5b). The modified GCE with GQD, due to the high surface area of this carbon material, the oxidation current of MTX is about 5 times more than the unmodified electrode. The surface area of the GCE/GQD (0.028 cm2) is 1.8 times higher than the unmodified electrode (0.015 cm2) according to the Randles–Sevcik equation. Therefore, the surface area of the electrode helps to increase the oxidation current of analyte and the drug measurement makes more sensitive. The conductivity of the GCE also is increased by electrochemical modification of a GCE containing AuNPs. As a result, the oxidation current of MTX is higher than that of the unmodified electrode and the GQD-modified electrode, and the oxidation current for MTX is equal to 45 μA. The increasing in oxidation current compared to other electrodes can be attributed to the larger surface area and the better conductivity of this electrode due to the presence of gold nanoparticles. Electro-polymerization of ANSA compound on the surface of the GCE/GQD/AuNPs caused the MTX oxidation current to be the highest current compared to other electrodes (75 μA). The latter case led to increase catalytic behavior of the modified electrode, which makes it more sensitive to MTX measurements. Although the surface area has not changed much in the presence of this organic compound, the electro-catalytic effects of organic compounds can be considered the most important factor in increasing the oxidation current of MTX. Therefore, the quantum dot graphene modified electrode, gold nanoparticles and organic compound have provided a perfect synergistic effect in MTX measurement. This effect is due to the increase in the surface area, conductivity and the improvement of the electro-catalytic properties of the GCE, so the MTX measurement was performed well by this sensor.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed as powerful technique to evaluate the improvement of the electrochemical behavior of the GCE toward used probe during different steps of modification by comparing the electron transfer resistance (Rct) given from the semicircle parts of obtained spectra. The values of Rct at the surfaces of GCE, GCE/GQD, GCE/GQD/AuNPs and GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA resulted from the Nyquist plots were 2.21, 1.13, 0.34 and 0.21KΩ, respectively (Fig. 6 ).

MTX drug has different currents and potentials at various pH depending on its functional group. The electrochemical behavior of this drug also varies at different pH solutions. Therefore, the electrochemical behavior of MTX on GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA surface at different pH of 0.04 M BR solution was investigated (Fig. 7a). Cyclic voltammograms of 50 μM MTX at different pH (3–9) indicates that the highest oxidation current is related to pH = 6, so this pH was selected as optimal condition (Fig. 7b). Also, the linear relationship between the oxidation peak potential of MTX and pH shows that Eq. (1) is established between the two factors (Fig. 7c). The obtained line slope from this equation shows that the number of electrons and protons exchanged in MTX oxidation not the same [54–56]. The width at half height of the reduction peak was ~88 mV which was near 1 mV for a 2-electron transfer reaction confirming an oxidation process involving the two electrons process.
(1)
E p
= − 0.030
pH + 0.967

The electrochemical behavior of 50 μM MTX at different scan rates is shown in Fig. 8a. The related MTX voltammograms GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA surface show that the oxidation current increases with increasing scan rate and a slight shift toward positive values indicate reversal reactions. The current diagram versus the square of scan rate is also shown in Fig. 8b. As the results show, the MTX oxidation on the GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA surface is a diffusion process and after placing the electrode in the solution containing the drug, MTX measurement is done quickly. The electron transfer coefficient was calculated from the potential curve versus (Fig. 8c) the current logarithm at scan rate 10 mV/s and by Eq. (2). The value of electron transfer coefficient with line slope equal to 0.16 was obtained 0.6.
(2)
Slope = 2.303
RT /
1 − α
nF

Different concentrations of MTX and analytical applications of the GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA were investigated by DPV method. The voltammograms of MTX different concentrations on the iron surface electrode in BR solution with pH = 6 are given in Fig. 9a. As the MTX concentration increases, its oxidation current also increases (Fig. 9b). The correlation between current and different concentrations of MTX in the range of 0.1–100 μM is linearly by Eq. (3). The detection limit for MTX and its quantitative limit are obtained 0.03 and 0.1 μM, respectively. The analytical values for MTX measurement were compared with other electrochemical studies. According to Table 1 , the results show that the detection limit and linear range of the current and MTX concentration correlation are comparable to previous reports. In this study, perfect results were obtained by a simple, inexpensive and easy method for MTX analysis. Therefore, it can be said that the proposed electrode is an ideal sensor for MTX measurements. The results show that the proposed method has a lower detection limit or comparable to other electrochemical techniques, so the synergistic effect of GQD and functionalized gold nanoparticle with ANSA has been successful in providing an electrochemical sensor for MTX-sensitive measurement.
(3)
I p
= 0.89 1C + 12.941

One of the most important parameters for an excellent electrochemical sensor is its suitable repeatability, reproducibility and stability. Therefore, to evaluate the repeatability of the modified electrode, a 10 μM concentration of MTX was measured on the GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA surface 5 consecutive times. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the MTX oxidation currents was obtained equal to 3.97 % after 5 straight times. The value obtained indicates that the electrode has an excellent repeatability. In order to evaluate the reproducibility of electrodes, 5 electrodes were modified with GQD/AuNPs/ANSA in the same way and 10 μM of MTX was measured in 0.04 M BR with pH = 6. The RSD value obtained from this test equals to 3.78 %, which indicates that the modified electrode has an excellent reproducibility. The stability of the modified electrode was evaluated after 1 month by MTX measurements every 6 days. During the MTX measurement the electrode was kept at 4 °C at different times. The results showed that after 1 month, the oxidation current of MTX decreased about 5.23 % compared to the first day, which shows that the prepared sensor has good stability.

MTX measurements were evaluated in the presence of compounds such as sodium chloride, iron nitrate, magnesium sulfate, L-cysteine, hydrogen peroxide, citric acid, dopamine and acetaminophen, on the GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA surface. The development is shown in Table 2 . According to the result, the oxidation current of MTX in the presence of different compounds slightly change (<5 %), indicating that these compounds do not have any interference effect on MTX measurements. Therefore, it can be said that the modified GCE have exceptional selectivity for MTX analysis.

Analysis of MTX in human serum samples was performed as real samples by standard addition method on the GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA surface. For this purpose, concentrations 1, 5 and 10 μM were added to human blood samples and then measurements were done with a modified electrode. The results are given in Table 3 . As can be seen, the recovery percentage from the MTX measurement in the real sample is 98to 102 %. These data indicate that the MTX measurement in the real sample is well done and this sensor has an acceptable capability for MTX analysis in complex media.

Highly sensitive electrochemical sensor with good repeatability, reproducibility and stability based on GCE modified with GCE/GQD/AuNPs/ANSA was obtained for MTX measurement. Also, the prepared sensor also has special selectivity. An acceptable detection limit of 0.1 was obtained for MTX measurements on the surface of this electrode. The high surface area of the modified GCE is due to the presence of GQD. This special surface area and suitable conductivity and electro-catalytic activity of the prepared sensor because of gold nanoparticles and thiol composition, improves the obtained results. These are great results due to the increased electrode surface area by GQD and AuNPs and their high conductivity, as well as the excellent catalytic properties of ANSA. Therefore, the performance of these electrodes shows that they can be used as electrochemical sensors. The capability of this sensor was completed by MTX analysis in the real sample.

Mrs. Maryam Zamani, Proposing the research, Conceptualization, Synthesis and characterization of GQD/AuNPs/ANSA, Acquiring various electrochemical data and Writing - Original Draft; Dr. Nahid Tavakkoli and Dr. Nasrin Soltani, Supervision, providing all resources and facilities and Validation of the work. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
